narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the unique strengths and challenges you've brought to your application, including your upper-middle-class family background and your choice to pursue full-time studies. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 45.8% chance of failure for you—high enough that it led to the rejection of your application to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic decile in your third year of school, which was in the lowest group compared to typical students who average around the middle of their class; this stood out as the biggest factor pushing the model toward expecting a bar failure. Your first-year decile, also at the bottom, reinforced this concern, making it the third-most influential factor in the same direction. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, slightly below the average of 3.21, actually worked in your favor more than expected, pulling the prediction toward success as the second-strongest influence. Similarly, your LSAT score of 31, though lower than the typical 36.7, and your family income bracket contributed positively, helping to offset some risks, while your full-time enrollment status had a small supportive role. Compared to other applicants, your early academic deciles were notably lower than average, which weighed heavily against you despite these strengths. We know this decision may feel disheartening, especially given the barriers many Black women face in higher education, but it's based solely on patterns from past bar exam data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted academic support, or exploring part-time programs or post-baccalaureate courses that have helped similar students improve their profiles. You're capable of turning this around—many have—and we encourage you to reapply after addressing these key areas. Keep pushing; your perseverance will shine through.
